fuzzy_prd:
We need the platform-agnostic contract layer for our location services, sitting between the app and whatever operating-system provider it runs on. The library needs four pieces: First, coordinate math — computing the straight-line surface distance between two GPS points and the initial heading from one to the other. The distance must be zero when both points are identical and symmetric when start/end are swapped. Bearing uses a signed convention where east-of-north is positive and west-of-north is negative. Second, the location reading value type — deserialized from the raw map the OS hands back. Latitude and longitude are mandatory; everything else has a default. Treat missing numeric fields the same way our sensor-data normalizer handles absent values. When a mandatory field is missing, fail fast with a neutral error line naming the missing field. Third, permission and accuracy catalogs — fixed ordered sets whose zero-based position in the list is the integer code exchanged with the platform. Codes 0-3 map to permission statuses; code 4 is a special 'unable to determine' status. Any other code is invalid. Fourth, domain errors — several categories accept an optional custom message (fall back to a default when none provided), two have a fixed description that never changes.

One small follow-up on the catalogs and the location value defaults, since the team asked how literal we want to be here. For permissions, the ordered mapping is 0=denied, 1=deniedForever, 2=whileInUse, 3=always. Code 4 is unableToDetermine in the catalog, but code 4 is invalid for permission_from_code since that operation only maps 0-3. Anything outside 0-3 should come back exactly as error=invalid_permission\nvalue=<n>.

For accuracy, the ordered set is 0=lowest, 1=low, 2=medium, 3=high, 4=best, 5=bestForNavigation, 6=reduced. Count=7.

And on the deserialized location reading, all numeric optional fields default to 0.0: accuracy, altitude, altitude_accuracy, heading, heading_accuracy, speed, speed_accuracy. floor defaults to null (absent/unset). is_mocked defaults to false.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: double distanceBetween(double startLatitude, double startLongitude, double endLatitude, double endLongitude)

trigger_keywords: what is the signed bearing convention
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: East of due north is positive, west of due north is negative. A due-west heading is reported as a negative quarter turn. When coordinates coincide, bearing is 0.0.

trigger_keywords: what are the two fixed-message error types
which error categories have no custom message
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: already_subscribed: 'The App is already listening to a stream of position updates. It is not possible to listen to more then one stream at the same time.' location_service_disabled: 'The location service on the device is disabled.'

trigger_keywords: what is the location reading summary format
summary line format for a location reading
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Exactly: 'Latitude: <lat>, Longitude: <lon>\n' — only latitude and longitude, no other fields.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: Treat missing numeric fields the same way our sensor-data normalizer handles absent values
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Optional numeric fields in the location reading deserialization (parse_position) default to 0.0 when absent from the input map.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check start.md or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/.

feature1_distance.json.description: Compute the great-circle distance in meters between two geographic coordinates, each given as a latitude/longitude pair in decimal degrees. The result is the shortest surface distance between the two points and is always non-negative; it is zero when both points are identical and is symmetric with respect to swapping the start and end points. The single output line is the distance in meters.

feature2_bearing.json.description: Compute the initial bearing (forward azimuth) in degrees for travel from a start coordinate to an end coordinate, each given as a latitude/longitude pair in decimal degrees. The bearing is the direction one must initially head along the great-circle path. It is zero when the two points coincide, and the result may be negative (east-of-north is positive, west-of-north is negative). The single output line is the bearing in degrees.

feature3_1_parse_errors.json.description: Deserialize a location reading from a key/value map. Latitude and longitude are mandatory. When either mandatory key is absent, deserialization fails fast; the failure is reported as a neutral error line naming the missing field rather than producing a partial reading.

feature3_2_parse_defaults.json.description: Deserialize a complete or partial location reading from a key/value map. Latitude and longitude are required; the timestamp is supplied as an integer count of milliseconds since the Unix epoch (UTC). All remaining numeric fields (accuracy, altitude, vertical accuracy, heading, heading accuracy, speed, speed accuracy) are optional and default to 0.0 when absent; the building floor is optional and is left unset (null) when absent; the mocked-provider flag defaults to false. The output lists every resolved field of the reading, one per line, including the timestamp re-expressed as epoch milliseconds.

cases.expected_output:
latitude=52.1
longitude=5.4
timestamp_ms=1000
accuracy=10.0
altitude=5.0
altitude_accuracy=2.0
heading=3.0
heading_accuracy=1.0
floor=2
speed=4.0
speed_accuracy=1.5
mocked=true

latitude=0.0
longitude=0.0
timestamp_ms=0
accuracy=0.0
altitude=0.0
altitude_accuracy=0.0
heading=0.0
heading_accuracy=0.0
floor=null
speed=0.0
speed_accuracy=0.0
mocked=false

feature4_position_equality.json.description: Compare two location readings for value equality. Two readings are equal only when every field matches: latitude, longitude, timestamp, accuracy, altitude, vertical accuracy, heading, heading accuracy, building floor, speed, speed accuracy and the mocked-provider flag. A difference in any single field makes the readings unequal, and equal readings always share the same hash while readings that differ in any field do not. The output reports both the equality verdict and whether the two hashes coincide.

feature5_position_summary.json.description: Produce a short human-readable summary of a location reading. The summary states the reading's latitude and longitude in a single line and intentionally omits the other fields. The output is exactly that summary line.

feature6_permission_from_code.json.description: Map an integer permission code coming from the platform into the canonical permission status token. Codes 0 through 3 map, in order, to the statuses denied, deniedForever, whileInUse and always. Any other integer is invalid: instead of guessing, the conversion fails and is reported as a neutral error line that echoes back the offending value.

feature7_1_permission_catalog.json.description: Report the catalog of permission statuses recognized by the system, in their canonical order. The output first states how many statuses exist, then lists each status token paired with its zero-based ordinal position; that ordinal is the contract used when permission values are exchanged as integers.

feature7_2_accuracy_catalog.json.description: Report the catalog of location-accuracy levels recognized by the system, in their canonical order from coarsest to finest plus the reduced level. The output first states how many levels exist, then lists each level token paired with its zero-based ordinal position.

feature8_1_error_messages.json.description: Render the human-readable description of a domain error condition. Several error categories accept an optional custom message: when a non-empty message is supplied it is used verbatim, and when the message is null or empty a category-specific default description is returned instead. The input names the error category and optionally carries a message; the output is the resulting description line.

cases.expected_output: Access to the location of the device is denied by the user.
Activity is missing. This might happen when running a certain function from the background that requires a UI element (e.g. requesting permissions or enabling the location services).

feature8_2_fixed_messages.json.description: Render the human-readable description of a domain error condition that has a single fixed description and accepts no custom message. The input names the error category; the output is its constant description line.

cases.expected_output: The App is already listening to a stream of position updates. It is not possible to listen to more then one stream at the same time.
The location service on the device is disabled.
